metadata.title: Claude Is Not a Mind Reader
metadata.topic: PROMPT ENGINEERING · Being Clear and Direct
sources: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial — Chapter 2: Being Clear and Direct

beats.narration_text: The framing that Claude is not a mind reader stuck with me, because my vague prompts really do get vague answers. I want to practice closing that gap. Help me: (1) take a lazy instruction like 'summarize this' and rewrite it with explicit audience, length, and format so the intent is unambiguous; (2) explain why spelling out the desired output shape beats hoping the model infers it; (3) show me one prompt that reads as clear to a human but is genuinely ambiguous to the model, and pinpoint the missing constraint.

props.command: The framing that Claude is not a mind reader stuck with me, because my vague prompts really do get vague answers. I want to practice closing that gap. Help me: (1) take a lazy instruction like 'summarize this' and rewrite it with explicit audience, length, and format so the intent is unambiguous; (2) explain why spelling out the desired output shape beats hoping the model infers it; (3) show me one prompt that reads as clear to a human but is genuinely ambiguous to the model, and pinpoint the missing constraint.
props.topic: PROMPT ENGINEERING · Being Clear and Direct
output: Claude has no context beyond what you literally write
Vague input → vague output — no guessing obligation
The Golden Rule: show your prompt to a new colleague
If they'd need to ask follow-up questions, rewrite it

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The Anthropic tutorial frames it this way: think of Claude as a new hire on their first day. Capable, eager to help, but with zero institutional context. They'll do exactly what you asked — which means if you asked for a summary and didn't specify length, format, or audience, they'll write whatever a summary means to them. The same prompt that frustrates you would frustrate any new employee. The problem isn't the model. It's the briefing.

props.artifactHeading: Claude executes the literal instruction — nothing more
artifactLines: Claude is capable and eager — but has zero institutional context
'Summarize this' → Claude writes whatever 'summary' means to it
No length, no format, no audience → Claude chooses defaults
The same vague brief would frustrate any new employee
The problem isn't the model — it's the specification

beats.narration_text: The tutorial's cleanest example: write a haiku about robots. Claude does it, adds 'Here is a haiku' before the poem. You didn't want the preamble. The fix: add 'Skip the preamble; go straight into the poem.' One sentence. Claude removes the preamble. This is the lesson in miniature — Claude isn't reading your mind about the preamble preference. Once you tell it, it complies immediately. Every formatting preference you have is one instruction away.

props.artifactHeading: One extra sentence eliminates every formatting complaint
artifactLines: Vague: 'Write a haiku about robots.' → Claude adds preamble
Direct: 'Write a haiku. Skip the preamble; go straight into the poem.'
Claude removes the preamble immediately — no iteration needed
Your preference was never ambiguous to you — state it once
Every formatting complaint is one explicit instruction away

beats.narration_text: Format specification works across every output type. 'Respond in JSON' — Claude responds in JSON. 'Use markdown headers' — headers appear. 'One sentence only' — you get one sentence. The tutorial tests this against open-ended questions too: 'Who is the best basketball player of all time?' Claude hedges. Add 'if you absolutely had to pick one player, who would it be?' — Claude picks one. The directness of your question determines the directness of the answer.

props.artifactHeading: Whatever format you want — name it explicitly
artifactLines: 'Respond in JSON' → Claude outputs valid JSON
'Use markdown headers' → structured headers appear
'One sentence only' → exactly one sentence
'Summarize in 3 bullet points for a non-technical audience' → done
The directness of your ask determines the directness of the answer

beats.narration_text: The Golden Rule of Clear Prompting from the Anthropic tutorial: show your prompt to a colleague who is new to the task, with no additional context. If they'd need to ask a clarifying question, your prompt is underspecified. Rewrite it until that new colleague could execute it exactly as you intend. That test catches more prompt failures than any model-specific trick. Claude is the new colleague. Write for them.

props.artifactTitle: The Golden Rule of Clear Prompting
props.artifactHeading: Test: could a new colleague execute this exactly as you intend?
artifactLines: Show your prompt to someone new — with no extra context
If they'd ask a clarifying question, rewrite the prompt
Clarifying questions reveal missing specification
Claude has no institutional context — only your words
Write prompts that need no interpretation. That's the whole lesson.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take a vague prompt you've used before and rewrite it using this method: 'Summarize the following article in exactly three bullet points. Each point should be one sentence. Write for a reader who has no background in the topic.' Then compare Claude's output to what you got with the vague version. The improvement will be immediate and attributable to nothing more than specification — no jailbreak, no model upgrade, no system prompt. Just words. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Summarize the following article in exactly three bullet points. Each point should be one sentence. Write for a reader with no background in the topic. [paste article here]
props.segment: Claude Is Not a Mind Reader
output: Claude produces exactly three bullet points
Each point is one sentence — no padding, no preamble
Compare to your vague prompt output — the gap is the specification

beats.narration_text: Claude Is Not a Mind Reader
